setting.note: A Zulu country pastor leaves the green hills above Ixopo in Natal for the vast city on the gold Reef, hunting for a son swallowed by its townships and shanties. The map holds the Johannesburg of the 1940s: black freehold at Sophiatown, the crowding of Alexandra, the shack settlement thrown up overnight at Orlando, the mines that pulled every man there, and the court where the story turns. The rural home that opens and closes the book lies far south in Natal, held on the map as a separate anchor.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Alan Paton, first published 1948. This book is mapped as a cited work: we store none of its text and reproduce no passages. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Cry, the Beloved Country, Alan Paton (1948). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The red earth of the eroded Natal hills the book grieves for, the land washed away where the young men have gone. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
